goal: CK metric follow-ups: Go receiver linking (method_declaration parent → receiver struct), Rust impl linking (fn inside impl_item parent → target struct), Ruby open-class aggregation (re-opened class scopes merge WMC/CBO/DIT/NOC)

summary.title: CK follow-ups: Ruby aggregation + Go receivers + Rust impls
summary.what: Closes the three CK follow-ups flagged in the initial CK port (commit 001e903). (1) Ruby open-class aggregation: ``_ruby_aggregated_groups`` merges same-simple-name Ruby scopes into logical-class groups; all four CK rules sum WMC and take max CBO across openings; DIT/NOC use simple-name-keyed indexes that aggregate automatically. (2) Go receiver linking: new ``Language.post_scan_adjust`` hook called once per file by Tree; ``Go.extract_name`` override walks type_declaration→type_spec→name to find struct names; ``Go.post_scan_adjust`` re-parents method_declaration callables under their receiver type (stripping pointer indirection). (3) Rust impl linking: ``Rust.post_scan_adjust`` walks impl_item nodes, records target types, and re-parents nested function_item callables to ``<file_stem>.<TargetType>``. Identifies impl_items by (start_byte, end_byte) span rather than id(node) because tree-sitter Python wraps nodes per-access. 482 tests pass.
summary.why: After the initial CK port, three pseudo-class shapes weren't handled: Ruby open-classes spread across files (each opening measured separately, not aggregated), Go method-receiver linkage (methods at file scope, not parented to struct), Rust impl-block methods (parented to anonymous impl_item scope). The user's instruction was to deal with these now so they don't get lost. Bundling all three in one intent because they share the same architectural concern (pseudo-class shape support) and benefit from the same testing approach (per-language WMC parity).
summary.user_goal: CK metric follow-ups: Go receiver linking (method_declaration parent → receiver struct), Rust impl linking (fn inside impl_item parent → target struct), Ruby open-class aggregation (re-opened class scopes merge WMC/CBO/DIT/NOC)

decisions.chose: Ruby aggregation at rule level via _ruby_aggregated_groups (not at Tree walker).
decisions.rationale: Ruby open-classes are a query-time concern: each opening is a real Scope record (different file:line). Aggregating at scan time would lose attribution information. Rule-level grouping keeps the substrate honest while letting CK metrics treat re-opened classes as one logical entity.

decisions.chose: Go and Rust adjustments via a new Language.post_scan_adjust(parse_result) classmethod hook.
decisions.rationale: Walker-internal Go/Rust logic would pollute the generic _walk with language-specific cases. A hook lets each grammar own its own post-processing, called once per file. Default returns parse_result unchanged so non-pseudo-class grammars pay zero cost.

decisions.chose: Identify impl_items by (start_byte, end_byte) byte-span, not id(node).
decisions.rationale: Tree-sitter Python bindings wrap the same underlying tree node in fresh Python wrappers per attribute access. id(node) from a top-down walk vs id(node.parent) returns different values for the same underlying node. Byte-span is stable across wrappers. Caught during initial Rust test failure.

decisions.chose: Re-parent callables in post_scan_adjust by constructing new Callable records via dataclasses.replace.
decisions.rationale: Callable records are frozen. We can't mutate parent/qualname in place. dataclasses.replace builds a new record; we also rewire callable_nodes dict keys to keep AST-node lookup working with the new qualname.

decisions.chose: Rust trait-impl ('impl Trait for S') parents methods to S (the target), not Trait.
decisions.rationale: The methods semantically belong to S (they're S's behaviour); Trait is just a contract. Matches legacy and matches Rust's mental model.

decisions.chose: Go type alias (type T int) returns '<anonymous>' from extract_name.
decisions.rationale: Aliases aren't classes; their type_declaration shouldn't be a class scope. Returning <anonymous> falls through CK iteration (anonymous classes contribute 0 to NOC; superclasses_of returns []). A cleaner fix would be to skip them entirely at scope-emission time, but that requires walker plumbing changes; the <anonymous> sentinel is the v2.0-window-acceptable workaround.

review_notes: 482 tests pass (was 475 post-CK-port: +2 Ruby aggregation, +5 Go/Rust linking, no regressions).
Two commits inside this intent: 463a6cf (Ruby aggregation, rule-level) + b3d8954 (Go/Rust linking, walker hook).
All four CK metrics now work view-native across all 9 OOP-pattern languages (Python, JS, TS, Java, C#, C++, Ruby, Go, Rust). Three remaining grammars (C, Julia, JS-non-class) don't have classes.

Language.post_scan_adjust is a new public hook on the Language ABC. Default returns parse_result unchanged; only Go and Rust override today. Future grammar-specific scan adjustments can follow the same pattern.
Port status unchanged at 11 view-native / 19 shim-wrapped — these are correctness follow-ups to already-ported rules, not new rule ports.

architectural_claims: Language.post_scan_adjust is the canonical hook for per-grammar record fix-up. Tree calls it once per file after the generic walker; it returns the (possibly rewritten) ParseResult.

Ruby's open-class semantic is a query-time concern. The Tree walker emits each opening as a separate Scope; CK rules aggregate via _ruby_aggregated_groups. This preserves per-opening attribution while enabling logical-class metrics.
Tree-sitter Python node-object identity isn't stable across accesses; use byte-spans (start_byte, end_byte) for cross-traversal identification.
behavioral_changes: Ruby same-simple-name classes across files aggregate in CK metric computations.
Go method_declaration callables are parented under their receiver struct's qualname.
Rust function_item callables inside impl blocks are parented under the impl's target type.
migration_notes: Third-party grammars that don't override post_scan_adjust inherit the default identity behaviour — fully backwards-compatible.
Code that introspects callables in Go/Rust files will now see methods under struct/impl-target qualnames instead of file-level. Consumers of qualname strings should expect the new shape.
